In women, especially those who are not pregnant, the follicle (i.e. the mammary gland that produces one egg) develops later on. In healthy women, the follicle develops so that the egg in it is viable as much as 13 weeks pregnant, provided you can maintain conception. (Epidemiologists note that over the first year of pregnancy, there are gaps of development around the ovary, in cases of women using birth control, potentially amplifying the chances of infection and stillbirth.) It is often believed that low levels of estrogen lead to failed pregnancies (especially in older women), but in healthy women it plays an important role for egg development and may even stimulate healing. What causes a failed pregnancy? A failed pregnancy is one whose eggs are not appropriately transferred into the follicle, beginning too early. A failed pregnancy is one whose eggs are not appropriately transferred into the follicle, born too early. A completely healthy, normal pregnancy ends when estrogen levels diminish, or what some doctors call "hypipause." This means that after one month, you are able to no longer produce new eggs with your body. Your body depletes estrogen for about 20 weeks, which is about half of your pregnancy. Your body, instead of birthing the baby, makes enough blood to fill the scrotal tube (the urethra) for the fertilized egg to travel. This matures slowly, taking as long as a few months to produce an egg. Then, the developing eggs are transferred into the adult follicle (called the womb) and attach to the cervix wall. Completely healthy women enter into a stage known as first-off-the-body or pre-uterula, since they are ovulating. Here, the process of healing that occurs with live birth is delayed by the doctor, often through the practice of an epidural. The pre-uterula baby is then transferred into a intact healthy vagina using a combination of local anesthesia and oral or intravenous drugs. This method is called an "assisted insemination," with an exception if there is a medical emergency. At a later stage, a cesarean is performed to complete the delivery, attaching the baby to the mother for support. This is an attempt to prevent fertilized eggs from moving into the follicle. The remainder of the pregnancy is completed naturally. Some healthy women, especially young women, get as much as 40 weeks pregnant and have miscarriages before the baby turns one. Whether it is inserted right before delivery (assisted insemination) or at about 24 weeks (cesarean), an epidural secures the vaginal lining muscles. This can determine whether a baby will be live or stillborn. For "live babies," the baby is put in the epidural before the volume of the egg has been taken in by the body, and before cells have finished mitigating in the follicle. For "stillborn" babies, the baby is put in the epidural before the lining has fully attached to the vaginal tract, and cells have started mitigating in the vaginal wall. The follicle never fully goes fallow, but never matures to the point that the egg in it is fully viable. This is when stillbirth is considered a tragedy. In the case of a pregnancy, you would actually be pregnant twice. So why was your baby premature? There are several reasons why your baby misses the ovulation to mature, and the circumstances of one woman will be different from that of another pregnant woman. Peak Rankings are making a name for themselves as a means of sorting possible team situations for squads. In my first go at it last year, it was a fantastic way to slot in a proverbial sleeper in Petr Mrazek for the Penguins and previously underutilized talents like Drew Doughty, Brent Burns and Seth Jones. While there are a number of perennial contenders who are riding some impressive streaks, the work done in this scoring system is starting to allow us to separate the wheat from the chaff and highlight the 12 teams to watch next season. Now, here is a bit of an explanation on how the rankings are compiled. In our system, arbitrary rosters bring out various kinds of aggregates of different categories. These pairs are given a percentage of their combined production and performance. Production is measured by points per 60 while team performance is measured in terms of goals against average. A score is given to the players and a few are given to the management. The former utilizes the depth within the next pairing and the amount of time a player has left to have a positive influence on the franchise. With the latter, certain factors are considered like flexibility, youth, and potential. It’s also important to note that there are no reverse scores or soft handed players in a particular system, as any tie in the lineup counts to nothing. Unless requested otherwise, a player is always ranked on account of their top-line production. Currently, the last ranking available is for April 22, and I have to remind you that we are six weeks away from when seeding actually means anything. A few teams have fallen and several new ones have risen, but things have become very muddled. As Waymo’s presentation showed, there are still several technical hurdles to clear before fully autonomous driving. “Standards and standards are put in place in different sectors to ensure we have more robust, more secure fully functioning connected cars, and that’s not happening,” Brilliant said, arguing that the features demonstrated in Tuesday’s demonstration are “the small steps and small modest improvements that can allow people to enjoy riding in connected cars.” Brilliant said he is highly concerned about cooperation between fleet companies and smartphone manufacturers. GPS usage from smart devices would require that they be connected into a car, an operation that is risky and prone to false positives, he said. “It’s the incentive structure that leads us to compromise by keeping one eye on safety and one eye on technological development, which can impact safety,” he said. “You have to have a full understanding of the consequences of dismissing it or removing it.” Though many of the police departments and the FCC’s challenges focus on driver behavior to assess the viability of fully autonomous driving, drivers have also said that many crashes involving self-driving cars occur because drivers are easily distracted by other moving objects on the road. woman, although the term is often associated with the ideology of masculinity. The rejection of gynocism has instead broadly been called “transagression”, founded on a view of a truly non-normative woman as one who can only choose to adopt certain male qualities. This view has often been deployed similarly to gynocism, for example commonly suggesting that members of the queer community cannot achieve masculinity or femininity (the usage of the term ‘sexual orientation’ taken by some to mean ‘sexual behaviour’, maintaining that the differences between men and women lie in certain social norms rather than genuine differences in biology, preferring to compare combinations of non-genetic features). Similarly, the use of such terms as ‘transsexuality’ has also received critical criticism, yet biocentrism has not condemned such language. After all, in a westernised society, we are no longer compelled to use words like ‘woman’ or ‘woman’. Shouldn’t we be careful in our use of language, and take into account the identity of the person in question? While’ gynocism is not considered a coherent ideology, it is related to a sub-genre of philosophy called misandry. As a result of this ideology, it is commonly argued that men are only able to perform ‘manhood’ in order to function within a gendered society and context. Though the term is often used as an insult, misandristism can lead to a certain inclination towards a gender ‘misplaced’ ideology, in which gender-nonconforming men do not simply cease to be men. While misandry posits that ‘men’ are able to live out a gender role within certain cultural parameters, these roles are only ‘males’ to the extent that they align with gendered cultural norms. Equally, transgender people who identify as male or female are only ‘male’ when performing this identity is socially aligned with gender roles, whereas they would be considered ‘gender misplaced’ if some aspect of their gender identity was on a ‘gender neutral’ basis. Likewise, ‘non-biological men’ are not men, since their even functioning in a ‘male role’ requires male privilege. As a contrast to gynocism, biocentrism contends that social identity categories reflect constructed cultural roles and gendered experiences. Consider, for example, the question of whether some people ‘need’ to be transgender. Biocentrism supposes that a person’s biological identity is only one facet of a much larger picture of that person’s life experiences and circumstances. Thus, if transgender people ‘want’ be transgender, it implies that they are rendered male or female (i.e. non-normative) by various factors other than shared biology or biological features. Having said that, biocentrism holds that the male and female categories exist only because various powerful, alienated and privileged individuals and groups have constructed male and female according to their own subjective interests. For example, biocentrism argues that a cis woman is born female, whereas a trans man is born a man. As well as raising fundamental questions about epistemological theory, biocentrism raises a number of questions about identity, sex, sexuality, childhood and masculinity/masculinity, which are unavoidably combined with ‘manhood’, ‘femininity’ and ‘self-identification’. Whether it engages ‘gender’ or notions of ‘masculinity’, biocentrism calls into question the validity of each of these concepts. This magical thinking promotes an ideology of ‘masculinity’, arguing that it is an essential, natural, socially determined and socially-valued entity, and that those who argue otherwise are culturally stunted or misplaced. In the case of sexuality, the critical theory suggests that masculinity entails a different social category than perceived biological sex, because of the inherent disparity between male and female expressions of sexuality. No definitive theoretical or empirical support for many of the claims made above is currently available, though some elements of these claims clearly warrant emphasis over others. Holding definitive beliefs in the absence of evidence or a sustained engagement with philosophy is always dangerous, particularly in cases of ideologies based on magical thinking. That being said, in the case of biocentrism it is understandable. Given that biocentrism has frequently been associated with the ideology of toxic-misogyny, it would be interesting to explore theoretical and empirical challenges specifically around ‘masculinity’, or otherwise associated with biocentrism. Biolocentrism often isolates trans women on the basis of their orientation. In a recent piece titled Trans Women Against Women, RadFem explored the relatively common phenomenon of trans people abusing women. As the SRT had been due to carry out the Section 17, procurement obligation since October 2016, it had 11 months to prepare tender permission and 12 months to start submitting bids. But the ministers agreed that once the SRT certified that it had met the conditions set out in sections 17(1) and 17(3) under the Public Service Regulations 1998, it will be deemed satisfied, making it eligible to enter into a procurement agreement for the SCRC. As part of a press briefing on civil service reform that was part of the lead-up to the Public Service Rally, a copy of changes made in the Public Service Act 1998 featured in the specific instructions that Finance Minister Lim Swee Chee had sent the SRT in relation to the rally. The document was a copy of a breach notice the PSC issued to the SRT for breaching the Section 17, procurement obligation. Destroying procurement obligation for bidding work on SCRC: Now SRT is free to bid As well as the undisclosed financial penalty for its breached procurement obligation, the SRT has been relieved of an award - one that was originally levied by the Transport Ministry for compliance with its bid on the CRL. The Transport Ministry will notify the recipients of the incentive in accordance with the budgetary resources available for this project. ADM Sakul Gopal The episode had begun only days before the selection of UTA to tender the SCRC, when the SRT had asked the Public Levelling Committee to cancel the procurement obligation for the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) report on the East Coast Rail Corridor. The SRT had to carry out a six-monthly review of its performance on the project and report back to the Transport Ministry on its progress in competition. On February 9, the Public Levelling Committee (PSC) gave the SRT a notice to suspend procurement obligations for the agency to review its work plan and assess its position in competition with competing agencies. Some of those teachers went on strike earlier this week and others were participating in job actions. But they are not going back to class and have not continued to organize strikes. "At this stage, we're just considering whether that would be considered," Thompson said Saturday morning, adding that it would be for each education ministry and Labour ministry to determine what the criteria are. "I don't want to go into the details any further here but I'm quite pleased we're catching up on this," Wynne told reporters in Hamilton Saturday. "I would expect this strike to be over rather quickly if there is a real willingness of all sides to come to the bargaining table," she said. Terry Ford, a high school English teacher, told CBC News she has been disappointed with the spin governments have taken lately to the dispute. "Without those negotiations it's just going to be a miserable back-to-school season all over again." Issues she's hoping to see for resolve are whether there will be enough teachers to teach the rigorous new curriculum. "I would hope that finally they've managed to come up with an agreement. I don't think they've managed to get to that point," Buckler said, adding that he's been waiting for information from the government and union to come out since a previous strike date in February 2011. He says he's been impressed with how much determination he's hearing from those who want to return to classes and that "it's going to happen." Watch interview with Jim Buckler, president of the Canadian Union of Public Employees. He says he's prepared to walk out of class early on Tuesday and resume strike action on Wednesday. "We hope the job action is going to be successful," Buckler said in an interview with CBC News. Martínez-Vega and his colleagues have recently described a plausible scenario of sea level rise for the coastal towns of the Okayama prefecture in Northern Japan and for New Orleans located on the Mississippi delta. Moreover, Martínez-Vega believes that "every coastal projection in the world is bound to overestimate future sea level rise and thus underestimate the vulnerability of coastlines." Most coastal projections are based on a response called radiative forcing, which refers to the warming of the ocean in response to an increase of CO2 in the atmosphere. Martínez-Vega points out this response is both important and under-appreciated. "Sea level rise understood as rising CO2 means warming ocean temperatures, greatly increased ice gain from rising coastal wave heights and much higher water loss from river subsidence," he explained. However, these factors are usually only talked about as an input for monumental cliff lines or other drainage measures. According to Martínez-Vega they are "usuallyperhaps even wronglyconsidered to mean little for coastal protection in general." He and his colleagues have developed a new way to track current and future sea level rise as the climate warms. "We show that in the future, sea level rise will reach approximately 5 meters as of today, consistent with the negative radiative forcing index as caused by reduced greenhouse gas emissions by the end of this century," Martínez-Vega explained. Reduced greenhouse gas emissions will reduce ocean heat, causing higher wave heights, and greater ice melting in the future. "Future CO2 concentrations do not fluctuate much in the model sea level, ice-loss, or river subsidence values, and it will clearly become a stronger driver of ice losshelping to explain the dramatic melting of ice caps and glaciers in recent decades." According to Paulyn Gerven, a Max and Planck Institute hydrologist and co-researcher on SWP2, the main aim of this study is to help inform modeling efforts considering the increase of CO2 in the atmosphere as the climate warms today. "The point of this study is to create a new SWP2 model that is robust to reduced RFs and air temperature to create a robust radiative forcing index constant for the future," she explained. "Our radiative forcing index is an important motivator in considering the sea level rise response to CO2 rise in more detail," said Martínez-Vega. The next step for Max/Planck Institute/PIK researchers will be to add future greenhouse gas emissions to the model and to determine what is a reasonable estimate for sea level rise, based on the effect of ice loss. voters, the researcher says. Whether registration mattered Another recent study from Cardiff University tackles what's called the "general election paradox." Has social media use among millennials had a significant impact on voting behavior, since the majority votes cast were liberal -- do they indicate a more liberal electorate? Studies examining the role of social media on voting behavior have generally presupposed that an individual, based on their affiliation with a political party, will simply vote without any influence from social media. One recent line of research, however, seems to contradict such an assumption. Professors Michael Cook and Anna Roberts looked at data from the 2012 general election to find out whether the deciding vote rested on someone's registration status or readiness to vote for any particular candidate. The most important factor was approval ratings of the major candidates, Barack Obama, Hillary Clinton and Mitt Romney, the professors say. They found that most voters did not shift if independents approved, or strongly approved, of two of the top three candidates with either the first conservative candidate or a third and less popular conservative candidate. However, they say, there is at least some evidence that certain conservative candidates, such as 2012 candidate Michele Bachmann who won the New Hampshire primary by less than a percentage point, were more popular among millennials than the major candidates. But whether independents, even those whom the campaign for Bachmann had called "fresh," had any impact on the winning outcome, early evidence is inconclusive. "Adoption does or does not matter," the paper concludes. Hard questions on social media While these studies and others help shed light on the debate over whether or not social media influences modern politics, they also raise tough questions. Although national political campaigns trying to capture young people's attention on social media outlets like Twitter or Facebook may seem to encourage turnout among younger voters, there is some evidence that they may put too much emphasis on getting young people to register to vote. There is also a debate about the effect of older voters turning out in force in order to counteract youth turnout. And the conclusions drawn by studies on social media use may also have assumed that the number of social media users was constant in time -- an assumption that has been challenged by some research. A few recent studies have found that it is growing over periods of time. While teenagers may be more likely to be active on social media, many other young people will simply exit the arena because they do not have enough time to devote to their social networks, Prof Fisher says. But "more and more young people are getting involved on social media," he says. "And they're probably voting earlier."<|endoftext_R9VQqF0Ag7|>Beijing: China's economic growth continued at a slightly sluggish pace in September, the national statistics institute said in revised data on Monday. China's economic expansion dipped in pace at 6.1 percent in the three months to September, down from a previous figure for a robust pace of 6.2 percent in the previous quarter. Overall growth solid Zhou Xiaoli, director of the National Bureau of Statistics, said despite the cool picture, affected by subdued demand for furniture, motor vehicles and industrial production, the economy continued to gain momentum in coming months. Meanwhile, underlying growth on an annual basis is projected to fall to 6.2 percent in 2016, down from a solid pace of 6.9 percent on an annual basis so far last year. Zhou said financial demand remained the biggest support, as provincial governments continued to extend loans to firms and construction projects proceeded with more support from the government. Slower growth in new investment coupled with attractive export conditions for firms are also contributing to the slower expansion, while commercial investment remained robust. A stable business environment combined with a stable distribution of income and private consumption growth are expected to generate even greater momentum, Zhou added. China's economic growth slowed from 6.7 percent to 6.5 percent in the previous quarter, but its annual pace was still forecast to be stronger than five percent in 2015. Third-quarter figures from the National Bureau of Statistics and local banks were well below forecast, with some considering the data revision pushing China on the economic downside last year. Zhou said industrial consumption remained stable and there are better prospects for expanding industrial production. Economists blamed the slowdown on the overseas construction industry, in addition to higher fuel and transportation prices, which had already weighed down consumer demand and industrial production. The image is of a blip, a phenomenon caused by a 4 billion light-years away galaxy falling into the black hole. The research team from NASA’s Stanford Infrared Astrophysics Laboratory, which works on the Green Bank telescope in Chile, has collected about 20 of these images in total. “When you have this many images, it’s mentally taxing to look for all the details that you can find and pinpoint one point in time,” said Mark Hayes, head of ESO’s black hole program, in a press briefing. To spot the blotches that are quite a distance away from us, the scientists have to look close in the telescope, but they also need a wider view to get more details. “Our biggest challenge was to get the widest view we could to look at this blip, so each point ends up being as far away from us as it can go,” Barrett said. Black holes showcase their stunning power whenever they exist anywhere in the Universe. As the galaxy passes, it uses its enormous gravitational pull to swallow matter particles, generating intense radiation in the process. These black hole giants resembling our Sun perk up in the Milky Way galaxy, “glamongous galaxies” are considered to be some of the brightest eclipses in the Universe, though they are relatively infrequent. The blip in the new distant image was part of one of these interactions. The mass sucked up by the black hole in the so-called brew was about 100 million times the mass of our Sun, ESO believes. The blip, which is approximately 100 times brighter than our Sun, happened a whopping 4.3 billion light-years ago then and thus lies 5.3 billion years back in time. Interestingly, material involved in the interaction escaped intact, not even the black hole itself. A cloud of gas and dust that had been floating in the Milky Way galaxy where the rogue black hole formed was caught in the act by the merger and then preserved forever. The color of the background is believed to reflect the texture of the blot, while the other subtle details such as the amount of diffraction thrown in appear to indicate the age of the object in the blot — somewhat like temperature readings. Black holes don’t just suck stuff off in spurts, like whales, but they can also spit stuff out in a stream. When much of the mass is already present in the reservoir of black, black holes can live for billions of years. But when a galaxy falls into one, the mass gets ejected out instantly. Is the truth behind the rise in spider popularity? It is time to celebrate a new beginning – the Museum of Modern Art (MOMA), which officially opened on Sunday, August 27. The building last underwent a radical renovation in 1921, when its founder Kaspar de Bronzeville described “Museo descverso” (a world with a doorknob). Now it is a world-famous museum, and is preparing to offer new venues after a $400 million renovation. The artists and designers who inhabit it have now arrived for the inaugural handover ceremony, with DJ and performers to regale those in attendance. But although the event is a chance to celebrate a new era, it is also a forum for the divergent stories in which a new era has begun anew. Recently the building’s architects Skidmore Telvey Jackson Associates (SEJA) discovered it had become infested with spiders. The news of the discovery has set off a slew of theories and statements speculating about why it happened. One of the most outrageous and most widely circulated explanations for the infestation is a theory that suggests the architects may have brought in spiders to “heat up creative inspiration” for the next museum tenant. Or, to add to the mythos of the theories, it was perhaps lead from lightbulbs during construction that led to the death on the site. So, why is the decision to design a property in response to public and private interest in spiders an untenable choice? Barry Dennick, president of SEJA believes the answer lies in the changing nature of buildings, which has seen architects transform a traditional emphasis on design for the factory landscape into an increasingly complex network of infrastructures. This means that much of what had been considered to be hardware dependances such as the water tank, the air conditioning unit and the telephone interface has now evolved into an intricate network associated with the particular industry or function being served by a building. In hospitals, for example, patient care is a central part of the building fabric. “Take the paediatric wards,” he explains, “and you also see the medical electronic readout, the radiistry and then all of those things have patient care networks.” Architecture focus is also moving away from what was once seen as the core functions of a design. “There are a lot more support systems in buildings, now,” Dennick points out, “so it’s more of a system oriented approach to architecture.” But Scott Spector, senior interior design leader at SEJA, doesn’t believe that the spooky tales of spiders running rampant are going to disappear any time soon. “I think there is an element of mystique in what ratmasters like to bring indoors,” he says. This report was commissioned to spark new research on the effect of violent media — something that researchers say would be necessary for developing sensible policy. It is one part of an ongoing effort by the Australian Government and the University of Technology Sydney to conduct such research. This research is used by the Australian Government to develop appropriate policy measures for the extent of the problem. To be clear: This report concluded that there isn’t enough evidence to draw strong conclusions about gun restrictions. We don’t know for sure, from this report, that all the evidence presented will ultimately lead to limits on guns. But it’s a possibility. The report proposed that “there may be a ‘violence life trajectory’ where violence is expected as children get older, and that this then extends into adulthood.” Guns are likely to be involved in a secondary way. One of the takeaways from the report is that existing violent content present in media, if not addressed critically, has the potential to promote violence. It’s not unexpected: it includes video games, specifically racing games and simulators. These simulations attach gameplay cues to what they do, particularly if they include violence. In fact, these sorts of games frequently presents violence as “subjective,” in the form of time-management, not a tactical or moral choice. That might be one of the reasons why these games may appeal to mass killers. Like Phennix and Fortnite, violence is no longer necessary to play, compete, or survive. But it is still pleasurable and emotionally rewarding. In other words, such “violence fantasies” can promote murder. The fantasies portray mass murder as a deliberate matter of joy, and not of typical human behavior. And if these fantasies become prevalent enough in media, creators and the audience may expect positive behavioral practices, and politicians and policymakers may adopt those practices. In the meantime, research on violence fantasies continues. One study, published in March of 2009 in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, found that “rather than predicting a immediate change in behavior, viewing [violence fantasies] was associated with an actual change in behavior 30% of the time.” Accidental violence may be inspired by a fantasy, but interventions that actually prevent intentional violence don’t completely stop it. Since then, more research into the effect of violence fantasies has emerged. A new study published last year in the Journal claims that a strong connection exists between violence fantasies and risky behaviors. More specifically, the study found that self-reported violence fantasies were correlated with measures of preference for alcohol, cigarettes, gambling, sex, eating disorders, and depression. So finding actual people with violence fantasies is a necessary requirement to identify meaningful indicators of individual risks. But it’s a risk. Rather, it comprises a set of proposals designed to act on some of the concerns expressed by MEPs both within and outside the scope of cybersecurity legislation and foster a broader, constructive debate. Wikicommons / Jakub Škowska Evidence-based cryptography Guided by the End-to-End Encryption campaign, this legislative proposal is designed to encourage the Council of the European Union of Telecommunications to develop specific, standardised standards for law enforcement agencies to assess the security of end-to-end encrypted messages obtained in their investigations and provide ongoing support to enable them to adequately conduct their legal and communication activities. Achieving the goals of this legislative proposal requires an effective policy framework, standardized training, the provision of technological innovations (for example, standardised phone numbers for end-to-end encrypted calling), the availability of modern and secure communication tools for law enforcement, and effective sustainable management tools. Law enforcement certainly welcomes an evidence-based assessment. The Swedish Institute of Traffic Management has been researching with collaboration by the Swedish police as well as the National Bureau of Investigation of Sweden. The EU has expressed concern regarding operational challenges from a comprehensive security perspective. End-to-end cryptography is a recognised technology choice for the protection of sensitive information in legal investigations and aims to strengthen the power of law enforcement agencies to achieve an effective confrontation with individuals engaging in crime and surveillance. Sensible standards for surveillance The use of surveillance as a second-step measure in criminal investigations and cyberattacks perpetrated by both criminals and state agencies is causing concern among researchers and law enforcement agencies. With the advent of end-to-end encryption, overall surveillance is seen as increasingly vulnerable and in urgent need of legislative oversight. Second-line surveillance – a term used to refer to the task of initial surveillance – often relies on an insecure investigation that exploits insecure monitoring systems, inaccurate reporting, and other information without a corresponding guarantee of control over the data. MEPs and others recognise that a delicate balance exists between the privacy interests of Internet users as well as the vulnerability to abuse. End-to-end encryption can help mitigate these tensions. However, there is active support among both cybercrime experts and civil society experts for greater transparency, which would provide additional guarantees of accountability with respect to the data gathered, and would allow easier privacy assurance to the research community and the broader public. In response to the limited use of end-to-end encryption, this legislative proposal aims to establish a framework for law enforcement agencies to establish a procedure for the collection and use of personal data as well as rules to ensure the respect of human rights principles and privacy protections in the conduct of surveillance. Tackling black markets According to reports on interception trends worldwide, 50% of interception in encrypted communications occurs through black markets. Black markets are commercial platforms that allow people to buy and sell various types of communications among other people. So far, they have been successful in bridging the gap between legal and illegal surveillance. However, over the years, state-sponsored hacking groups have used criminal schemes to commit identity theft and nation state apparatuses to sell reverse-structored end-to-end encrypted communications on dark markets. In this context, there is a demand for clarification on how to mitigate the risks associated with black market interception. This legislative proposal would create a mandatory standardised database of all encrypted communication messages, including stored data related to interception, to minimise adverse impacts to privacy of individuals. It would also provide for voluntary verification and the ability to legally prevent trading with black market purchasers. Conclusion The joint parliamentary committee on cybersecurity is an ambitious initiative. To be effective it needs to engage in a broad debate that touches on complex legal, technical, and political questions. At the same time, this type of debate should be open to encourage the continued use of encryption by citizens across countries and, more crucially perhaps, to foster cooperation among public authorities. Overall, there is space for multi-party discussion about encryption and discussions have begun in earnest – but parliamentarians are facing strong opinion differences between encryption experts. On the one hand, academic-based research makes clear that standards-based systems in free communication should not be pursued at the expense of allowing for flawed surveillance, while, on the other, the community promotes a civil society-led approach to striking a balance between the protection of privacy and security. This diversity of opinion is at odds with the core mandate of the committee, which explicitly seeks expert opinion on encrypted communications protocols. If experts are to provide meaningful oversight, they will need to engage closely with voices represented in the encryption knowledge base and make efforts to develop a shared encryption policy. Second, there was no obvious lag caused by sleep deprivation, since it is well known that sleep deprivation is related to greater sleep-derived excited state variability in response to challenging tasks [27] , especially with an increasing number of cognitive tasks [28] . New Jersey is one of the most popular destinations in the country for refugees piling over its border, with many fleeing violence, persecution and economic collapse that have transformed lives elsewhere in the world. Twelve refugees will be coming to the state this year to live in one of 15 resettlement sites run by the federal government and New Jersey, Debra Sexton, the state’s director of refugees, told NJlive.com in an email late Wednesday. Sexton said the state is aiming to resettle at least 1,000 refugees by the end of 2016, an increase of almost 40 percent over the last projected numbers drawn up for 2014 by the United States Agency for International Development. “While we’re really focused on arriving in New Jersey, a good deal of our time here is keeping an eye toward the next year,” Sexton said. The state has already gathered all of the training materials it will need for preparing eight resetees, Sexton added. The announcement came just days after the Supreme Court placed new restrictions on the pace of refugee resettlement. The court upheld the Obama administration’s proposal that more than 40,000 refugee applications should be approved each year, tied in part to the roughly 11,400 who were repatriated in the nine months before the February restrictions were put into place. But the decision by the court has urged the administration to keep to that level in the interest of keeping the country safe. “(The decision) does not create a national policy of closing one’s borders and stalling when that policy is the only feasible remedy,” the court said. Four refugee applications to the state have been approved, with the remainder being submitted still awaiting approval, Sexton said. “We’re pretty confident that we are going to have the applications for the remaining four slots approved by the end of the month.” Sexton added that the applications for resettlement facilities and contractors will be considered through a separate process. The plans are moving forward even as the identity of the assigned refugees is in doubt. The last two refugees to be admitted were Somalis, but eight of the 11 are Africans. The government of Eritrea recently sent its top official to the state, David Kwanguma, to verify his credentials. Even with the restrictions in place, there has been little interest in New Jersey anyway. low inflation. In the 1990s this process was undertaken by a group of countries that dominated monetary policy. These countries are the fab five. A large amount of quantitative easing also came from the fab five. This led to sharp falls in interest rates around the world and not just in the Western countries. It also changed the shape of the banking system. In Brazil, Japan and Australia the banking system was dismantled and therefore interest rates were allowed to rise and fiscal policy was much less stringent. The fall in interest rates in the decades leading up to the last few years had unintended consequences. These range from missed opportunities to excessive tightening of strategic level policy. The fall of interest rates has created a giant debt service. To understand the debt of the current period it is important to understand the relationship between credit conditions and interest rates. When credit conditions are tight they reduce potential productive capacity of the economy. They tend to reduce the operating efficiency of the economy, they prevent new supplies of credit from being fully exploited and hence reduce the growth of investment and employment unless there is a speculative boom. When interest rates are low then productivity growth is effective only if there is an increasing supply of capital, better goods and services and greater new supplies of credit. Therefore both credit conditions and interest rates contribute to slow growth. The fall in interest rates can be understood in this context as a major element in the current deterioration of the global economic outlook. Obviously the population that has been discouraged from lending activity are households and firms for which borrowing activity is the key way to access new supplies of credit. The fall in interest rates is not just something that has led to slow economic growth. Interest rate changes can also lead to a dysfunctional financial system. Banks set interest rates by leveraging their reserves. If they use the greater loss bearing reserves (favouring higher rates) the loan flow increases financing financial institutions. In the current period banks have had excess lending reserves. They would need high rates in order to have reduced loss bearing reserves. Further falls in interest rates would have created an increase in equity reserves and a reduction in loss bearing reserves. With low rates the money supply tends to expand. This reduces the value of the asset base and level of investment. With higher rates the value of loans as an asset would be greater. Therefore capital formation, construction and other investments would reduce or fall. The sharp fall in interest rates has led to a fall in bank asset values. This has led to capital flight. The financial crisis has led to a very slow recovery in global economic growth. In reality the opposite outcome has occurred, increased capital flight and the sharp fall in interest rates have actually slowed economic growth. I would argue that the reasons for the slow recovery in economic growth associated with the fall in interest rates and increased capital flight are not imagination. There has been a shift in the global composition of assets and a consequent recovery of production capacity. In particular there was a reduction in unemployment, a recuperation of lost manufacturing production capacity and an increase in employment. The rising infrastructure base has coincided with both an increase in bank deposits and a rebalancing of the asset base towards the financing of more debt. The global change in macroeconomic conditions in the last five years has led to reduction in the current expected level of growth followed by a sharp slowdown. In some cases there has been a change in what can be defined as the expected basket of goods and services that firms, households or banks expect to earn income on. For some, this meant that high export countries or industries that were often operating at a high work intensity would have lost competitiveness and therefore seen their profits fall. In some cases it has meant that financial institutions that have provided large amounts of credit in the past to support growth are now providing credit at a reduced pace. This has meant that those firms that had been expanding had to consider restructuring. There have been decisions by firms and individuals to reduce their level of investment in the purchase of consumption generating assets such as housing or equipment. Many individuals have also made the decision to sell the goods and services they just bought at a discreet price or sell them later. Those who have decided to reduce in consumption also experienced a lower income. Consumption and employment have also slowed in response to both internal and external changes in macroeconomic conditions. So there has also been a reduction in the size and intensity of capital projects. There is also an increase in capital flight from the banking system. With capital flight there is another challenge to interest rates, there is growing capital flight and interest rates are set to reflect a new level reached in other markets. So the changing composition of the balance sheet, the falling intensity of the manufacturing base and the falling growth of investment have themselves contributed to the slowing of global economic growth. What happens is that once the balance sheet reaches a certain level and banks decide the amount they are lending will be reduced by a percentage as opposed to quarterly increases. The view nonetheless arose that the fiscal framework was the only logical and appropriate lever for supporting Ontario's transportation infrastructure. Fiscal discipline also followed strong lobbying from government stakeholders for a simpler, more flexible way of financing infrastructure projects. So while the framework was able to determine the interest rates at which debt would be paid, the public was concerned about whether interest rates were responsibly applied. Story continues below advertisement For example, the interest rate applied on the additional $426 million worth of transit debt acquired by the Ontario government in 2011-12 was a fixed interest rate of 4.5 per cent for 20 years. As a comparison, the federal debt framework had proposed a fixed interest rate set at 5.5 per cent for 30 years and gradual increases later on. The public could argue that the Ottawa framework was more generous because early debt payments are subject to higher interest rates, and it could argue that lower interest rates favour borrowing for infrastructure projects in Ontario. Taken together, the 2011-12 fiscal framework refinanced about $10.4-billion of debt from Ontario's existing infrastructure portfolio. However, it also took on new debt with significant transportation implications, bringing the total amount involved to $16.8-billion. The new transit-related debt tends to be debt for which the interest-rate differential is significantly higher. While Ontario has refinanced about $1.2-billion of new transit debt between 2009 and 2012 (and there is more transit debt in the pipeline), the net refinancings currently total less than $6 billion. It is clear that, if the province wishes to prefinance the payment of interest on debt raised through the new sales tax, it must borrow a significantly different amount from the portion it has taken on under the fiscal framework. This issue is significant because based on the public accountability assumptions in the framework, the amount of eligible transit debt financed eventually depends on a number of factors. These include the provincial interest rate for this new transit-related debt, which is expected to be higher compared to the fixed interest rate set in the framework. There is also a prolonged period in which interest rates are expected to be lower because of a) excess borrowing for infrastructure and b) the decline in aggregate revenues brought on by the reduction in demand for transit services (for example, lower passenger volume and fewer trucks and buses on the roads). The Globe has recently reported that Infrastructure Ontario (the agency responsible for the allocation of the sales-tax funds) is incurring $1.2-billion in interest on $457-million worth of consolidated debt imposed on the province's operating transit corporations, including OC Transpo. This provincial interest rate reflects the cost of existing levels of transit-related debt. It is double the rate of interest paid on revenues generated by the provincial sales tax. (It also assumes that there will be sufficient additional sales-tax revenue to cover the total amount of debt and the total cost of debt incurred.) Story continues below advertisement Putting part of this money up for bid would have the effect of repudiating part of the $16-billion worth of debt acquired to date, and that is exactly what some public finance types are calling for. I think it is fair that some of this money be made available as a reserve, not just for the renewal transportation investment, but to compensate the communities, municipalities and citizens affected by it. That isn't to say that we can't support public and common ownership of entities like OC Transpo. There are valid arguments to be made about the benefits of public-sector ownership and public control. However, any transit refinancing needs to be done in a transparent way and open to competition. That financing can be, and is, done in a manner that protects the public interest, avoids imposing additional costs on taxpayers, and supports the province's renewal transportation strategy. Oleg Kupeverbaev/Shutterstock In some areas like wetlands or rainforests birds (and reptiles) are particularly vulnerable to extreme heat stress. Scientists in France published a comprehensive study on heat stress in October showing that a wide variety of birds are highly vulnerable to long periods of hot day temperatures and cold temperatures at night. Most birds survive between 72.6 to 76.7 degrees Fahrenheit with minimal stress, but the scientists identified a climatic hot spot for heat stress that peaked at 65.5 degrees Celsius in June/July 2013. That's too hot for birds and also too hot for falcons (and their food). Birds aren't the only animals to experience heat stress. In June, researchers in southern South Africa published a report that found that some animals spent more than a year at the hot spot inside the Victoria Falls National Park. Why do bats survive longer? In some cases, it's due to skin adaptations to heat stress: bats develop a wooly pattern to mimic the predation behavior of a neighboring species, which feeds on coyotes first when they are young. So if the coyote mauls a bat, it waits until its incubation finishes to cause serious damage. M. Kulikov/Shutterstock "Heat can lead to reduced growth and development, reduced reproductive development, and pushed back gestation timing in bats and rodents," the authors said in their report. "This study helps clarify which aspects of these adaptations are most important, which plants and animals have these adaptations, and how they can best adapt to heat stress." The scientists studied various species at dozens of sites ranging from pristine forests to glacial melt, resulting in comprehensive findings on 12 million square meters (72 million square feet) of plants and animals exposed to severe heat. "Heat stress is an important issue, and these findings highlight the importance of applying concepts described in the dryland section of this review to more distant regions," the authors said. In simple terms, the reserve requirement is the minimum available resources needed to lend a certain amount of money. However, in the finance industry, the money lent is usually set more requirements in securities. In reality, the reserve requirement indicates the amount of capital needed for lending. In simple terms, the reserve requirement carries the risk that sometimes the loan can’t be paid in an emergency. In the credit union–promoter relationship, the reserve requirement of a loan enables lenders to pay their loans for a specific time with the least amount of capital available at a certain stage. In general, the reserve requirement is set as low as possible. This is made possible by special requirements imposed by agreement between a credit union and a lender (finitor) or the credit lender itself. Reserve requirement (VRD) Let’s assume that the credit lender, Smith, is providing a loan to another credit union, Voces, Lisbon, Portugal. The interest rate of the credit lender (Smith) will be €900.00 for 5 years, meaning that borrower will get a return of €7,750.00. Assuming, on the other hand, that the other credit union has offered a 50 percent discount on its interest rate means it will have to pay €4,971.00 for the loan. The reserve requirement seeks to ensure a relationship between the lender and the borrower where they can meet the lender’s interest rate with the amount of capital available at their disposal. Because the borrower’s interest rate’s €864.50 and the capital needed is €430,000, the reserve requirement is 150.00/4.971, which translates into 150.00 percent and 4.971 respectively. Fundamentally, the reserve requirement is calculated strictly according to agreements made between the creditor and borrower. Interbank lending is a financial market that is structured on the principles of management and credit. Meaning, the borrower and creditor are matched in such a way that it provides real debt securities at a quinetic rate. In return, the borrower gets more money for a specific time. Sometimes it needs the extra money, but it should charge the lower interest rate on collateral (cash) for the higher interest rate of lending. Peer-to-peer lending market The interbank market in contrast to the bank market engages more in borrower-to-peer lending. As mentioned earlier, there are the three main variables that influence the interest rate of loans and deposits offered by a lender: ■ Funding scope. ■ Access to credit. ■ The reserve requirement. We can say that the credit scope is the margin on which debt service can be paid for the debtor. The type of collateral asset determines the interest rate required by the creditor. However, how is the rate determined? 3 factors determining collateral interest rate variations What determines the collateral interest rate level in general? Three factors play a role as a result of determining the interest offered by each collateral type. These factors are the amount of private debt, quality of credit and credit market rate. High debt: When a firm has a lot of debt, there is an increased probability that it will default on its debts. Because of high debt levels, the loan becomes less profitable based on the prime interest rate. Hence, it becomes more profitable to repay the loan than paying a advance to the borrower. Good credit rating: A recipient of a loan with a negative credit rating is in great need of collateral such as corporate bonds for a reasonable interest rate. The good credit rating is the third factor of the collateral interest rate. Strong credit market: A appreciable amount of credit entering the market makes it safe for the creditor and also enables it to raise funds for the investors in a bid to repay its debt. In order to change collateral interest rate levels, the interbank market must perform an interest rate discovery. It sets the interest rate that the creditor should be paying as low as possible so as to maintain a positive market for the collateral. About the interest rate discovery As mentioned earlier, it is difficult to adjust interest rates as it depends on the quinetic rate in an agreement. A greater degree of system structure flexibility is desired because alignment between product areas is critically important and changes to development focus in one part of a system can impact on the other. Modules can be created in order to drive this more effectively while maintaining the integrity of the life cycle structure, focused on quality and structured to ensure gains in efficiency. Cash flow: EBITDA is understood to be a difficult, recurring accounting error that is unable to be achieved on an ongoing monthly basis. It will typically be captured indirectly on short run financial statements. Problems: S&P EBITDA: EBITDA, when S&P is used to determine a current valuation of publicly traded companies, when there are clearly several ways to determine what a company is worth. Instead management is caught in contrasting the path the S&P analysts take every few years to determine how much a company is worth and make the error of deducting excessive expenses to improve EBITDA, if the number is inflated it can give a false positive. S&P EBITDA, when S&P is used to determine a current valuation of publicly traded companies, when there are clearly several ways to determine what a company is worth. Instead management is caught in reinventing the path the S&P analysts take every few years to determine how much a company is worth and make the error of deducting unnecessary expenses to improve EBITDA, if the number is inflated it can give a false positive. Dilution is not EBITDA. It's completely separate and the majority of the money that contractors lose due to missed contract delivery is dilution. When a project falls through it is important to account for the dustution costs, which include insurance payments, legal fees and other costs that you don't want in your books. In the short term you avoid costs you haven't planned for but in the long term you lose because you will, during the recovery, pay more costs directly to your suppliers that you can't afford. Dilution is generally not subject to measurement and Bill Burken of the National Coalition Against Deficit Restructuring estimates companies lose $184 billion each year with no existing regulatory body to make the accounting decision. Restructuring can be a very costly activity; managers are generally unaware of the consequences. Solutions: Recompuring a contract to add a default, or to remove excessive upside costs, should be the initial step a company should take when project delivery deadlines are missed. A company should evaluate whether reworking the scope of a contract is necessary to meet the lender's requirements. When dilution payments are leveraged or related to employee pensions, companies are encouraged to collect and pool them so that employees can continue to collect their pension at the lowest applicable rate. Accumulated cost information: Cost information is budget data throughout a project life cycle, but the initial estimates are used to calculate the project's value. This allows management to identify and manage their budgetary obligations, and target then maximize attainment. Certain cost information can also be used to create a full project outlook. Companies use cost data to guide their long term liabilities. Revenues, revenue and profits are generally calculated by subtracting the cost of a purchased product or service from the original purchase price. This is called the gross profit margin. This is divided by the number of items that were sold to subtract total revenue before gross losses are calculated and the true result of the service purchase agreement is considered. The companies that supply products are ultimately the owners of cost information as long as the cost is not charged to the customer to begin with. Cost information is generally collected in computer software called SAP for use by other companies and in standardized tables that are made available to all vendors, providing a common source when benchmarking the value of a project scope. In this instance the executive project team relies on the owners of the original information to determine the financing strategy. Considerations: Project processes need to be in place to capture, document and centralize cost information at every stage of the project cycle, so realistic budgetary levels can be applied as consistently and accurately as possible throughout the project. This includes all contract amounts, including background line items, cash advances, and strategic marketing and contracting bids. The recommendation is to capture cost information once a project gets underway and items such as salaries should be recorded with as rigor as possible before construction begins. Accomplishments should be established to transparently address priorities across a project cycle. Directors should prefer SAP standard data. It’s probably a misconception audiences move straight past. In fact they are very much invested in Ace as he wakes up from a coma and tries to understand the world in front of him. As a result the action sets sides more common tropes of Sci-Fi, familiar locations like the American city of New Orleans get a brief mention which has some subtle foreshadowing along the way. Incorporating such tropes in the film goes a long way to giving the films credibility and familiar concepts which in turn helps keep the film interesting and fun. Future does get a big grand scale display that heavily concentrates on big transitions and large spaces and it’s futuristic concepts connect much better than they initially seem. The film uses the formula from the first film in a creative new fashion, a structure that helps keep the audience on their toes to watch the behaviour of characters in far flung locations and to enjoy the surprising results. Moviecraft (3.5/4) When it comes to science fiction sequels, in terms of the options there are currently currently available it does not get much better than this. Bringing this British Sci-Fi surreal comedy to the stage in multiple locations with a space frame ranging from 10-20 years is a seriously brilliant idea. Key narration is anchored by Johnny Depp and Jeeves is portrayed in a bar performance by bagged actor. and film director. Stephen Ward. It blends the typical quirky pub street humour with a dry humour that really adds to the films broad appeal. That dry satire is let down by the absence of wit, which is unfortunate but unsurprising given Johnny Depp’s lack of dramatic theatre. I

In this example the smart contract has a 50:50 split of rewards, with each chain getting a 50% voting rate and the total reward of the validator split 50:50. The fixed amount of REDs distributed in the specific matching order of participants to the validators ensures that if the network goes through a hard fork, participants holding all sets of rewards have the incentive to participate. Of course, this incentive is modified by the fact that there are no incentives to work on the old chain. This means that in the event of two chains merging we only have one chain that can function after the hard fork. If something goes wrong during block production, the validators can refuse to sign out new blocks, resulting in a hardfork. In the example described in the introduction, each chain has a different set of validators and independently had to agree on the block height. Here in our protocol we still have the same smart contract, making it a lot more likely that if there is no attack, the chain can continue on its current path in this case. As I said before, the node is designed to validate until the block height, so it can be stress tested to its utmost limit. My question to everyone then: how would you choose between a drastic hard fork that makes it easy to attack and split the chain as soon as possible, a naive hardfork that is too easy to be exploited, or a hardfork that is safe and can work to everyone’s benefit? Being employed as an affiliate professor in such a programme is a privilege: I find a more monotonous career alternative boring. I think that others working in my field could benefit from a stint in such programmes as well. They would be challenged to engage with the numinous qualities of soft displays while still pursuing the cerebral approach of science. The real problem lies is with the inability of some scientists to engage with soft displays, and the unspoken mistrustful of scientific techniques studied and understood by some astronomers and engineers, compounded by the inability to use them. Indeed, I often feel more comfortable at home spread out on a pub patio than in a tunnel of ethereal mirrors towards a nebula to take part in a demonstration of the first principles of astrophysics. A few colleagues and I were present at an official launch meeting for a new astronomy journal at a conference last autumn. A special poster was made to commemorate the occasion and, after it went to the gallery, I demonstrated a device I had been developing in my lab the previous year. It was a device so sensitive to the internal environment of particles – cosmic rays coated in a liquid silica gel, and atomic nuclei trapped in a magnetic-cold shell – that it could isolate and measure the light properties of their contents. All in attendance looked at my little experiment with awe at thousands of stars. They could not describe the display: some of them trembled with excitement, while others were just giggling. Human outreach I hope that one day, we will be able to apply what we have learned in the service of humanity. But until then, our habits and practical worries make us look like introverted scientists.
